shortDescription: Phacoemulsification cataract removal with an intraocular lens implant, performed as a day case in Korean eye clinics.

sections.overview: Cataract surgery removes the eye's clouded natural lens and replaces it with a clear artificial intraocular lens (IOL), and Korea is a practical destination because its eye clinics combine modern phacoemulsification equipment, premium-lens options, and an efficient exam-to-surgery workflow within a short trip. The standard technique is phacoemulsification: a surgeon makes a small incision, uses ultrasound energy to break up and aspirate the emulsified lens, and positions the folded IOL inside the lens capsule. The procedure is usually a day case performed under topical or local anaesthesia, taking roughly fifteen to thirty minutes per eye. The choice of intraocular lens shapes both the cost and the visual outcome. A monofocal lens provides clear vision at a single focal distance and typically leaves a patient relying on glasses for either near or distance tasks. Premium options such as multifocal, extended-depth-of-focus (EDOF), and toric lenses aim to reduce spectacle dependence and to correct astigmatism, and they are generally paid out of pocket. Some clinics also offer femtosecond laser-assisted steps, which add to the price. International patients are evaluated by a Korean ophthalmology team that confirms the diagnosis and measures the eye before recommending a lens. A typical stay of three to five days covers the pre-operative examination, surgery, and a next-day review, and many travellers choose to treat both eyes during a single visit. This sidecar explains who is a candidate, how the procedure runs, what recovery looks like, and how Korean pricing is structured so prospective patients can plan with realistic expectations rather than guarantees.
sections.candidacy: Cataract surgery is generally considered when a cataract has progressed enough to interfere with everyday function rather than simply being present on an eye scan. Common signs include glare or haloes around lights, a gradual dimming or yellowing of colours, difficulty reading, and trouble driving, particularly at night. A Korean ophthalmology team confirms candidacy through a clinical examination and biometry, which measures the length and curvature of the eye to calculate the correct intraocular lens power. During that assessment the clinical team also looks for other eye conditions, because factors such as corneal health, retinal disease, or prior eye surgery can influence which lens type is appropriate and what visual result is realistic. Patients considering premium multifocal or toric lenses are usually screened more carefully, since these lenses suit some eyes better than others. General health is reviewed as well, as conditions like diabetes may affect healing and follow-up planning. Because the live diagnosis depends on an in-person examination, an overseas patient's suitability and recommended lens are confirmed on arrival rather than promised in advance. Anyone unsure whether their cataract warrants surgery can share existing eye records with the clinical team for an initial view before travelling.
sections.procedure_steps: On arrival, international patients typically complete a pre-operative examination and biometry, often on the same day, so the surgical team can finalise the intraocular lens choice. Before surgery, eye drops dilate the pupil and numb the surface, since the operation is usually performed under topical or local anaesthesia with the patient awake but comfortable. In the operating room, the surgeon makes a small incision at the edge of the cornea and opens the front of the lens capsule. Using phacoemulsification, an ultrasonic probe breaks the clouded lens into fragments that are gently suctioned away. The folded intraocular lens is then inserted through the same small incision and positioned within the natural lens capsule, where it unfolds into place. The incision is typically self-sealing and often needs no stitches. Where offered, femtosecond laser-assisted steps may be used to create some of the incisions or to soften the lens before removal. The active surgical time is commonly around fifteen to thirty minutes per eye. Afterwards a shield or patch may protect the eye, and the patient rests briefly before being discharged the same day with eye drops and instructions. A review the following day checks healing and early vision. When both eyes are being treated in one trip, the second eye is usually scheduled a short interval after the first once the team is satisfied with initial recovery.
sections.recovery_timeline: Recovery from cataract surgery is generally quick, though it unfolds over several stages. Immediately after the procedure vision is often blurry and the eye may feel gritty or watery, which is expected as the numbing drops wear off. Many patients notice useful vision returning within roughly seventy-two hours, while the eye continues to settle over the following weeks as any minor swelling resolves and the prescription stabilises. A next-day review confirms the lens position and healing, which is one reason a stay of three to five days is suggested. Prescribed antibiotic and anti-inflammatory eye drops are typically used for a few weeks to support healing and reduce infection risk. During early recovery patients are usually advised to avoid rubbing the eye, heavy lifting, swimming, and dusty or dirty environments, and to wear any protective shield as directed, especially while sleeping. Because air travel does not generally affect a healing eye through pressure, many patients can fly home within a few days once the clinical team confirms the eye is settling, but the timing should be agreed with the treating ophthalmologist rather than assumed. Final spectacle prescriptions, if glasses are still needed, are usually checked once vision has stabilised over the weeks after surgery. Outcomes vary by case, depending on the eye's starting condition and the lens chosen, so patients should follow their individual aftercare plan.
sections.cost_breakdown: In Korea, cataract surgery commonly ranges from roughly USD 750 to USD 4,500 per eye, and KmedTour lists an indicative band of about USD 2,000 to USD 5,000 for a typical international package. The main driver of cost is the intraocular lens. A standard monofocal lens sits at the lower end, while premium multifocal, extended-depth-of-focus, and toric lenses raise the price because they aim to reduce spectacle dependence or correct astigmatism. Adding femtosecond laser-assisted steps increases cost further. Korea's national insurance can offset part of a monofocal procedure for residents, but international self-pay patients should plan around full clinic pricing. A typical quote should clarify what is included, such as the pre-operative examination, biometry, the surgeon's fee, the lens itself, facility charges, and the next-day follow-up, and what may be billed separately, such as premium lens upgrades, additional laser steps, medications, or treatment of the second eye. Travelling patients should also budget for accommodation and local transport across the three-to-five-day stay. One practical saving is the value-added tax (VAT) refund: as overseas visitors, international patients can often reclaim eligible VAT at the airport on departure, so retaining receipts and asking the clinic about the refund procedure is worthwhile. Because final pricing depends on the lens and any laser options chosen after examination, a written itemised estimate from the clinic is the most reliable figure to plan against.
sections.why_korea: Korea is a well-established destination for eye care, supported by a government-backed international healthcare programme run through the Korea Health Industry Development Institute under the Ministry of Health and Welfare. For cataract patients this translates into clinics that use current phacoemulsification systems, offer a full range of intraocular lenses including premium and toric options, and frequently provide femtosecond laser-assisted techniques. A practical advantage for overseas patients is the streamlined workflow: many clinics can complete the pre-operative examination, biometry, and surgery within a compressed schedule, sometimes beginning evaluation and treatment on the same day, which keeps the trip short. A stay of three to five days is usually enough to cover the examination, surgery, and next-day review, and many travellers complete both eyes in a single visit. International patient services, including coordination and language support, are a defined part of the national programme rather than an ad-hoc arrangement. The value-added tax refund available to overseas visitors can also reduce the effective cost. As with any surgery, results vary by case, so the role of a Korean eye team is to confirm the diagnosis, recommend a suitable lens, and set realistic expectations rather than to promise a specific visual outcome.

faqs.q: Should I choose a monofocal or a premium intraocular lens?
faqs.a: A monofocal lens gives clear vision at one distance and usually means relying on glasses for either near or far tasks, and it is the most affordable option. Premium lenses, such as multifocal, extended-depth-of-focus, or toric designs, aim to reduce spectacle dependence or correct astigmatism, but cost more and are not suited to every eye. The right choice depends on your eye measurements, lifestyle, and the Korean ophthalmology team's assessment after your examination, so the decision is usually confirmed on arrival rather than in advance.

faqs.q: Is cataract surgery painful, and am I awake during it?
faqs.a: The procedure is usually performed under topical or local anaesthesia, so you stay awake but the eye is numbed with drops and should not be painful. Most patients feel pressure or see moving lights rather than discomfort. The active surgery commonly takes about fifteen to thirty minutes per eye. Afterwards the eye may feel gritty or watery for a short time as the numbing wears off, which is expected. Tell your clinical team if you feel anxious, as they can discuss options to help you stay comfortable.

faqs.q: Can I have both eyes treated in one trip to Korea?
faqs.a: Many patients do treat both eyes during a single visit. The eyes are usually operated on a short interval apart rather than on the same day, so the team can confirm that the first eye is settling before proceeding with the second. A stay of three to five days is often enough to cover the examination, both procedures, and follow-up reviews. Whether this suits you depends on your eyes and the clinical team's judgement after the pre-operative examination, so the schedule is confirmed once you are assessed in Korea.

faqs.q: When will my vision clear, and when is it safe to fly home?
faqs.a: Vision is often blurry immediately after surgery and many patients notice useful vision within roughly seventy-two hours, with the eye continuing to settle over the following weeks. A next-day review checks healing, which is one reason a three-to-five-day stay is suggested. Air travel does not generally affect a healing eye through cabin pressure, so many patients can fly home within a few days, but the timing should be confirmed by your treating ophthalmologist rather than assumed, since recovery varies by case.

faqs.q: What does the price include, and what costs extra?
faqs.a: A typical Korean quote should clarify what is included, such as the pre-operative examination, biometry, the surgeon's fee, the lens, facility charges, and the next-day follow-up. Costs that may be billed separately often include premium lens upgrades, femtosecond laser-assisted steps, medications, and treatment of the second eye. Korean pricing commonly runs from about USD 750 to USD 4,500 per eye depending on the lens and laser options. Ask the clinic for a written itemised estimate after your examination, since the lens choice is the main driver of the final figure.

faqs.q: Can international patients claim a VAT refund?
faqs.a: Overseas visitors can often reclaim eligible value-added tax (VAT) when leaving Korea, which can reduce the effective cost of treatment. To benefit, keep your receipts and ask the clinic in advance about the medical VAT refund procedure and the documents you will need at the airport on departure. Refund eligibility and amounts depend on the specific services and current rules, so confirm the details with the clinic and at the airport tax-refund desk rather than assuming a fixed amount.

references.label: Medical Korea — official international healthcare program (KHIDI, Ministry of Health & Welfare)
